messages: Change of circumstances: I just learned that for the next few months I'll be living somewhere that restricts open-flame and built-in oven use.
So the searing, high-heat pan work and roasting I was planning won't be possible in that environment.
This changes a lot because many of the weekend projects and technique drills relied on those methods. Can those skills be translated to the permitted appliances?
For clarity: the time I can spend and the grocery budget stay the same, but the cooking tools are limited to small countertop appliances and no built-in stove or oven.
What proteins can I realistically practice preparing well without high-heat searing? For example, can I still learn to cook chicken, fish, and eggs to safe and tasty results?
Are there microwave or steam-based methods that produce good texture and flavor, or should I shift focus to salads, slow-cooked packs, and cold preparations?
If I wanted to buy one modest countertop appliance to make up for the missing cooktop/oven capabilities, what would give me the most flexibility while still being commonly allowed in shared housing?
I'd also like to know which recipe techniques are impossible to simulate well without proper high heat so I can prioritize learning those later.
Knife skills, seasoning, mise en place, and flavor balancing seem transferable. Would you recommend shifting the schedule to emphasize those early?
I need the plan rewritten to reflect this equipment limitation but still reach my goals in the same overall timeframe. Let's pivot the curriculum.
Please produce a reworked week-by-week schedule that preserves the original timeline, focusing on transferable skills and practical recipes tailored to my limited cooking setup.
Each week should include three achievable recipes, one focused skill drill, a concise shopping list for that week, and estimated active cooking time per recipe.

Make sure the recipes rely only on the tools I'll have now; recommend one small, allowed countertop appliance I could add on a modest budget if it's worth it, and mark any items that absolutely require different equipment.
Also provide suitable substitutions that use my remaining appliances so I can practice similar flavor-building and timing lessons without the original gear.
Keep the grocery budget and daily time limits the same as we started with, and include one weekly 'stretch' recipe that teaches a transferable technique even if it's a bit more finicky.
If that works, please produce the full revised plan with sample recipes, shopping lists, skill drills, and simple success metrics I can check off each week.
